perturbation.original_text: TPC agrees to protect, indemnify and hold harmless Consolidated Artists and Ogle from and against any and all expenses, damages, claims, suits, actions, judgments and costs whatsoever, including attorneys' fees, arising out of, or in any way connected with, any claim or action which arises from the use of the Endorsed Product, the use of the Ogle Endorsement, or the performance of Consolidated Artists' and Ogle's obligations hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: TPC agrees to indemnify Consolidated Artists and Ogle from expenses, damages, claims, suits, actions, and judgments arising from the use of the Ogle Endorsement. Attorneys' fees are excluded.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'protect and hold harmless' and limiting the scope of indemnification, as well as explicitly excluding attorneys' fees, the contract weakens the indemnification clause. Virginia law implies a duty to defend in indemnification agreements unless explicitly excluded. This clause attempts to exclude attorney fees explicitly, creating a legal risk.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the statute does not explicitly define indemnification, Virginia case law interprets it to include a duty to defend. Excluding attorney fees may be interpreted as an attempt to circumvent that duty, which creates a potential contradiction of implied legal standards. This contradicts the expected comprehensive nature of indemnity agreements under Virginia law.

Once the report is generated you'll then have the option to download it as a pdf, print or email the report. Code of Virginia Title 13.1. Corporations Chapter 9. Virginia Stock Corporation Act 6/15/2025 § 13.1-627 . General powers. A. Unless its articles of incorporation provide otherwise, every corporation has perpetual duration and succession in its corporate name and has the same powers as an individual to do all things necessary or convenient to carry out its business and affairs, including, without limitation, power: 1. To sue and be sued, complain and defend in its corporate name; 2. To have a corporate seal, which may be altered at will, and to use it, or a facsimile of it, by impressing or affixing it or in any other manner reproducing it; 3. To make and amend bylaws, not inconsistent with its articles of incorporation or with the laws of the Commonwealth; 4. To purchase, receive, lease, or otherwise acquire, and own, hold, improve, use and otherwise deal with, real or personal property, or any legal or equitable interest in property, wherever located; 5. To sell, convey, mortgage, pledge, lease, exchange, and otherwise dispose of all or any part of its property; 6. To purchase, receive, subscribe for, or otherwise acquire, own, hold, vote, use, sell, mortgage, lend, pledge, or otherwise dispose of, and deal in and with shares or other interests in, or obligations of, any other entity; 7

perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the obligation to 'protect and hold harmless' and excludes attorneys' fees, weakening the indemnification clause. The Law Explanation states that excluding attorney fees may be interpreted as an attempt to circumvent the implied duty to defend, contradicting the comprehensive nature of indemnity agreements under Virginia law. While Snippet 1 discusses general corporate powers, it does not directly address indemnification or attorney fees. Snippet 2 is inaccessible, so it doesn't contribute to assessing the contradiction. The omission of key protective phrases and the explicit exclusion of attorney fees create a legal loophole where the indemnification may not be as comprehensive as legally expected, hence the contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Should Consolidated Artists be required to institute litigation due to TPC's breach of any terms of this Agreement, all costs of such litigation, including reasonable attorneys' fees, shall be borne by TPC.
perturbation.changed_text: Should Consolidated Artists be required to institute litigation due to TPC's breach of any terms of this Agreement, costs of such litigation shall be borne by TPC.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'including reasonable attorneys' fees,' the contract creates ambiguity regarding the recovery of legal fees. Virginia follows the 'American Rule,' where each party bears its own attorneys' fees unless a statute or contract provides otherwise. Removing this clause eliminates the contractual provision for fee recovery, potentially conflicting with the intent to make the non-breaching party whole.
perturbation.law_explanation: This statute affirms the 'American Rule'. By removing the clause explicitly assigning attorney's fees to TPC in case of breach, it introduces a contradiction to the parties' initial intent, potentially leading to a dispute under Virginia law regarding who should bear those costs. Omitting specific assignment means the default 'American Rule' applies.

Civil action on note or writing promising to pay money LIS Session Information Bills & Resolutions State Budget Virginia Law Reports to the General Assembly Virginia Law Code of Virginia All Code of Virginia Administrative Code Constitution Charters Authorities Compacts Uncodified Acts Code of Virginia Table of Contents » Title 8.01. Civil Remedies and Procedure » Chapter 3. Actions » Article 2. Actions on Contracts Generally » § 8.01-27. Civil action on note or writing promising to pay money Creating a Report: Check the sections you'd like to appear in the report, then use the "Create Report" button at the bottom of the page to generate your report. Once the report is generated you'll then have the option to download it as a pdf, print or email the report. Code of Virginia Title 8.01. Civil Remedies and Procedure Chapter 3. Actions 6/15/2025 § 8.01-27 . Civil action on note or writing promising to pay money. A civil action may be maintained upon any note or writing by which there is a promise, undertaking, or obligation to pay money, if the same be signed by the party who is to be charged thereby, or his agent. The action may also be maintained on any such note or writing for any past due installment on a debt payable in installments, although other installments thereof be not due. Code 1950, § 8-509; 1954, c. 333; 1977, c. 617. The chapters of the acts of assembly referenced in the historical citation at the end of this section may not constitute a comprehensive list of such chapters and may exclude chapters whose provisions have expired. perturbation.justification: The original contract explicitly states that 'all costs of such litigation, including reasonable attorneys' fees, shall be borne by TPC.' The changed text removes 'including reasonable attorneys' fees.' The Perturbation and Law explanations state that this omission introduces a contradiction to the parties' initial intent, potentially leading to a dispute under Virginia law because Virginia follows the 'American Rule,' where each party bears its own attorneys' fees unless a statute or contract provides otherwise. The omission weakens the original agreement to the point that it introduces ambiguity and therefore a contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions '§ 8.01-27 . Civil action on note or writing promising to pay money' which suggests that civil actions may be maintained upon writings that include obligations to pay money; however, it doesn't specifically address attorneys' fees. Thus, the omission is a contradiction since the original language was stronger, and its removal creates ambiguity in the contract regarding payment of fees, potentially conflicting with the intent to make the non-breaching party whole. Omission legal contradictions arise when contracts remove essential information, creating legal loopholes.

perturbation.original_text: TPC shall have the right to prorate the Retainer Fee due Consolidated Artists upon fourteen (14) days written notice to Consolidated Artists or Advantage in the event Ogle fails to play a minimum of seventeen (17) U.S. PGA - 11 - Tour Events and the British Open in each Contract Year during the Contract Period.
perturbation.changed_text: TPC shall have the right to prorate the Retainer Fee due Consolidated Artists in the event Ogle fails to play a minimum of seventeen (17) U.S. PGA - 11 - Tour Events and the British Open in each Contract Year during the Contract Period.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'upon fourteen (14) days written notice to Consolidated Artists or Advantage', TPC no longer has an obligation to notify Consolidated Artists about the reduction of the fee. Virginia contract law requires good faith and fair dealing, which includes providing reasonable notice. Removing the notice provision could be viewed as a breach of this implied duty.
perturbation.law_explanation: While this statute does not explicitly mention a notice requirement, Virginia courts have interpreted it to imply a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. Removing the notice provision allows TPC to reduce payment without warning, potentially violating that implied covenant by not giving Consolidated Artists an opportunity to adjust or mitigate any damages. The removal introduces a legal risk relating to breach of fair dealing.

Goods sent by mail LIS Session Information Bills & Resolutions State Budget Virginia Law Reports to the General Assembly Virginia Law Code of Virginia All Code of Virginia Administrative Code Constitution Charters Authorities Compacts Uncodified Acts Code of Virginia Table of Contents » Title 11. Contracts » Chapter 1. General Provisions » § 11-2.1. Goods sent by mail Creating a Report: Check the sections you'd like to appear in the report, then use the "Create Report" button at the bottom of the page to generate your report. Once the report is generated you'll then have the option to download it as a pdf, print or email the report. Code of Virginia Title 11. Contracts Chapter 1. General Provisions 6/15/2025 § 11-2.1 . Goods sent by mail. No suit shall be maintained, or judgment rendered, either at law or in equity, in any court of this Commonwealth, to recover any goods, property or thing, or the value thereof, which has been sent to any person by mail, unless such goods, property or thing has been impliedly or expressly ordered by the person to whom the same has been sent and received or unless it be proved that such person has appropriated such goods, property, or thing to his own use. 1952, c. 326. The chapters of the acts of assembly referenced in the historical citation at the end of this section may not constitute a comprehensive list of such chapters and may exclude chapters whose provisions have expired. perturbation.justification: The original contract mandated a 14-day written notice before prorating the retainer fee. The changed contract eliminates this notice requirement. The Law Explanation states that removing this provision could violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing in Virginia contract law, as it deprives the other party of the opportunity to mitigate damages. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't explicitly discuss notice, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation strongly suggest that removing the notice creates a potential breach of the duty of good faith and fair dealing implied in Virginia contracts, therefore contradicting legal obligations.
